text: Records of Strange People from the Huai and Yangtze River Regions** **Compiled by Wu Shu during the Song Dynasty** **Table of Contents** - **Volume One** - Tang Prince of Ning - Flower Maiden - Shen Fen - Nie Shidao - Li Mengfu - Li Sheng - Pan Yi - Chen Yunsheng - Chen Shu - Sima Jiao - Liu Tonggui - Shi Gonghao - Dong Shaoyan

### Volume 2 #### Geng Xiansheng #### Wife of Zhang Xun #### Zhang Biao #### Yu Da #### Jiang Chushi #### Qian Chushi #### Recluse of Runzhou #### Strange Person of Jiankang #### Scholar of Hongzhou #### Old Man of Hangzhou #### Fisherman of Santan #### Soldier of Xuanchou ### Volume 1 #### Prince Ning of the Tang Dynasty Prince Ning was skilled in painting horses. In the Huayue Building, there was a mural depicting "Six Horses Rolling in Dust." Emperor Minghuang loved a white horse with a jade-like face, but later it was lost, leaving only five horses. #### Hua Gu Song Danfu had a special art, transforming peonies into thousands of varieties. Emperor Minghuang summoned him to Lishan, where he planted ten thousand peonies of different colors and patterns. He was called Hua Gu. According to this book, all the stories are about people from the Southern Tang Dynasty, except these two entries, which are from the time of Emperor Minghuang of the Tang Dynasty. When checked against later Song and Yuan dynasty texts, they all agree. Therefore, they are retained in their original form and listed at the beginning of the volume. #### Shen Fen At the end of the Tang Dynasty, Shen Fen, a low-ranking government official, retired and lived a life of virtue and pleasure. He had two concubines. One day, he told them, "If I die, can you cry for me?" The concubines were very surprised and said, "How could you say such an unlucky thing?" He insisted on asking them, and they replied, "If you say so, how can we not cry?" Shen Fen said, "Now you try to cry, and I want to see it." The concubines initially refused, but when he persisted, they fled. Shen Fen caught and hit them, and they had no choice but to say, "You just sit on the bed." Shen Fen followed his instructions. The two concubines then cried on either side of him, holding their sleeves together. After they finished crying, they looked at him and found that he had already died.

聂师道聂绍元附 聂师道，歙州人。年轻时就喜欢道教。唐末，于涛担任歙州刺史，他的哥哥于方外是道士，住在南山中，聂师道经常去拜访他。于涛时常去拜访于方外，甚至在处理州政时，都会向他咨询，因此将山命名为“问政山”。吴朝因为聂师道长期居住在这座山上，所以称他为“问政先生”。起初，于方外在山中时，州民很少信奉他。但聂师道到来后，信奉他的人逐渐增多。聂师道与一位朋友同行，来到一家旅店，朋友因热病而痛苦不堪，村中已经没有医药。有人教病人说：“如果能吃一点不干净的东西，可能会好一些。”当病情危急时，又劝他吃。病人面有难色。聂师道对他说：“事情紧急了，何必在这里犹豫，我先尝尝。”于是他尝试吃了一些。人们被他的义举感动，病人也吃了，病情果然立刻好转。后来，给事中裴枢担任歙州刺史，正值唐末，诏令无法执行。宣州的田頵和池州的陶雅举兵攻打歙州，围城数月，歙州多次被攻破，后来粮食耗尽，援兵断绝，城中商议投降，但城中已经杀死了许多外军，没有人敢出城传达消息。聂师道自愿出城。裴枢说：“你是一位道士，怎么能进入战乱之中呢？请换上衣服去。”聂师道说：“我已经接受了道教的教导和方法，不容易换衣服。”于是他用绳子从城墙上缒下，出城传达了投降的消息。

The two generals were initially very surprised, but when they spoke with him, they were greatly pleased and said, "Truly a Taoist priest!" After the oath and agreement were established, he was sent back to the city. When the time came, if the general had not yet completed his duties, he wished to extend the agreement, and again ordered Master Shidao to explain it. People thought he was being inconsistent, and all were concerned for him, but Master Shidao showed no difficulty. When he met the two generals again, they all said, "Nothing is impossible, only follow your orders." At that time, the people in the city were saved, thanks to Master Shidao's efforts. The Wu founder heard of his reputation and summoned him to Guangling, where he built the Purple Extreme Palace for him to reside. One night, a group of bandits broke into his residence, taking everything, even the utensils. Master Shidao said to them, "You are bandits, taking my wealth to save yourselves from hunger and cold. What use is this to you?" He then led them to a secluded room, gave them all his gold and silk, and told them, "You should leave from a certain place; there will be no patrols, so you can avoid trouble." The bandits followed his instructions and escaped unharmed. Later, the Wu court sent Master Shidao to Longhu Mountain to perform rituals. On the way, he encountered a group of bandits who attacked him, intending to harm him. One of them looked at Master Shidao closely and said to his companions, "Do not harm the master." He ordered them to return everything they had taken, and the bandits all complied. They then told Master Shidao, "I am the one who was a bandit at the Purple Extreme Palace in Yangzhou years ago. I am grateful for your utmost kindness and now repay you." Master Shidao later died in Guangling. At that time, an envoy was being sent to Hunan and Hubei, and upon his return, he met Master Shidao and asked, "Why are you here?" Master Shidao replied, "The court sent me to perform rituals at Nanyue." The envoy believed him. When he entered the Wu territory, he only then realized that Master Shidao had died.

Zhao Yuan, the nephew of Nie Shidao, entered the Dao at a young age. He had a gentle and elegant appearance and was skilled in writing. He died in his early twenties. At first, Zhao Yuan was already seriously ill, and four cranes gathered on the roof of the place where he lived. When he died, people saw five cranes flying away into the sky. Li Mengfu was a person who frequently visited the marketplaces of Hongzhou. He was in his early twenties, short, fair-skinned, and beautiful, like a jade person. He behaved freely and unrestrainedly, always wearing flowers in his hair, visiting all the wine shops in the city, singing loudly, and getting drunk. Many well-intentioned people invited him to drink, and sometimes asked him to compose lyrics. He composed lyrics immediately upon request, though done without much thought, each piece had its own charm. During the reign of Zhong Fu, the governor of Hongzhou, he was going to punish Li Mengfu for his reckless behavior and influence on others. Li Mengfu composed over ten poems while in prison, and one of the lines was: "Wearing flowers and drinking alcohol do not cause trouble, singing while chopping wood and fishing do not interfere with time." Zhong Fu did not punish him. Later, the governor of Guilin, Li Qiong, sent an envoy to Hongzhou, saying that Li Mengfu was his brother, and asked to release him. Zhong Fu ordered a search for Li Mengfu in the city's inns, and the person said, "He did not return last night."

The fate of this person remains unknown. According to *The Elegant Words of the Prefectural Hall*, Li Mengfu was an individual of unknown origin. During the early years of the Kai Ping era of the Later Liang dynasty, Zhong Fu governed Hongzhou. He would often drink with commoners and recite poetry in a unrestrained and free-spirited manner. He once used a fishing rod with a fish hanging from it to attract the attention of fishermen in the market, selling his poems. Enthusiasts eagerly bought them. After receiving money, he would go to a tavern. His poems numbered in the thousands and were widely circulated in the southern regions. Here are a few examples: "The bell sounds of a village temple echo across distant shoals, a half-moon sets over the mountains ahead. Slowly rowing back to the bay, waves reflect the colorful dawn." Another poem reads: "The fishermen are happy to come, the magistrate has completed his duties and sits by the river. Coconut and yulania cups carved from wood, a pot of steamed mandarin fish fills the table." Each time he touched ice to water, steam rose from his body. After the Zhong family passed away, his whereabouts also became unknown. This account is appended here.

Li Sheng, a scholar, once traveled to the western mountains of Hongzhou and gathered with several others, including the recluse Lu Qi, for a drinking session on a snowy night. One of the participants remarked, "The snow is so heavy, it's impossible to go out." Li Sheng replied, "Where do you need to go? I can go there for you." Someone then said, "I have some books in Xingzi. Can you fetch them for me?" Li Sheng agreed and left. The gathering had not yet ended when he returned with the books. Xingzi is about 300 miles away from the western mountains. There was a Taoist priest in the traveler's rest house who had previously shown no respect to Li Sheng. Li Sheng said, "I cannot kill him, but I want to make him afraid." One day, the Taoist priest closed the door and went to sleep. Li Sheng sent a servant to fetch a dagger and knock on the door. The Taoist priest woke up and saw a dagger inserted into his pillow, which still appeared to be moving. From then on, the Taoist priest became afraid and started to treat Li Sheng with respect.

Pan Yi Pan Yi was the son of Pan Peng, a Censor of Dali. When he was young, he lived in Hezhou, where he collected firewood from Jiling Mountain to support his parents. Once, while traveling to Jinling, he moored his boat at the mouth of the Qinhuai River. An old man asked to be taken across the river with him, and Pan Yi, respecting the old man's age, agreed. It was snowing heavily, and Pan Yi bought wine to drink with the old man. By the time they reached the middle of the river, the wine ran out, and Pan Yi regretted having too little to get drunk. The old man said, "I also have wine." He untied his headband, took a small gourd from his hair, and poured it out, and the wine never ran out. When they reached the shore, the old man said to Pan Yi, "You are filial and have a natural affinity for the Dao, you can be taught." He then taught Pan Yi Daoist techniques. From that day on, Pan Yi's actions were extraordinary, and the world called him "Pan the Immortal." Once, when he visited a house, he saw many fallen leaves in the pond and told the owner, "This can be used for a game." He had the leaves scooped out and placed on the ground, and they turned into fish according to their size. When he threw them back into the water, the leaves returned to their original state. There was a man named Kuai Mao who asked Pan Yi to perform a trick to entertain the guests. Pan Yi saw a blacksmith shop in front of the house and asked for their hammer to use as a prop.

Upon arriving, Pan Yi took out a small knife and cut something very finely until it was completely consumed, astonishing the guests. He then said, "Borrowed objects should not be damaged." He reassembled the object, and the chopping block returned to its original state. Once, he took out an old square cloth from his sleeve and told someone, "Do not underestimate this. It should only be borrowed in times of urgent need. Others cannot obtain it." He then used it to cover his face, walked back several steps, and it disappeared. He could recite books by heart without having seen them before, and by starting with a single word, he could recite the entire book. He was fully aware of all the annotations and corrections within the text. These feats were not always remembered or recounted in full. He eventually died due to illness. According to the *History of Southern Tang* by Ma Ling and Lu You, both have a biography of Pan Yi, describing his travels between the Yangtze and Huai rivers, where he referred to himself as a "wild guest." He once relied on the governor of Haizhou, Zheng Kuangguo, who did not treat him with much respect. In his traveling case, there were two tin balls that shone like a white rainbow. Anyone who touched them would have their body and head severed. His actions were similar to those of a swordsman. After Zheng Kuangguo learned of his abilities, he recommended him to the founding emperor of Southern Tang. Pan Yi was summoned to reside in the Purple Extreme Palace and died a few years later. At the same time, there was another Pan Yi who once presented a recipe for a divine elixir to the founding emperor. Both Pan Yis are completely different, and this information is being added as an appendix.

**Chen Yusheng** Chen Yusheng was from Raozhou, and people called him Chen BaiNian. From a young age, he was quiet and reserved, and he loved the Dao. His family had long been engaged in hunting with bows and arrows, but Yusheng alone did not eat their meat, nor did he converse with others. At the age of ten, he went to Longhu Mountain to enter the Dao, retreating into deep seclusion, where few people ever saw him. If any of his family members did see him, they would flee without looking back. During the Tianyou era, people saw him at Machu Mountain in Fuzhou. Calculating the time since he had left home, it had been seventy years, yet his appearance remained unchanged. During the Shengyuan era, the governor Wei Quanfeng had long known of his extraordinary abilities, and so he invited him to the prefectural capital, placing him alone in a room; sometimes, he would lose track of him. Once, while sitting at night, Wei said to him, "The oranges of Fengcheng are delicious; I miss them greatly." Yusheng replied, "There is now a boat of oranges docked at Laogang [which is also called Fengcheng Port, as noted in volume 21,129 of the *Dadian*]. I will go and get them for you." The port was fifteen li from the city, and shortly after he departed, he returned, carrying a cloth bag with several hundred oranges, and they ate them together. Once, Wei had a marriage ceremony and needed to buy gold, but the gold in the prefecture was scarce and insufficient for the occasion, so he scolded his subordinates. Yusheng said, "Do not be angry; I can do this for you." He then took thick paper, coated it with medicine, and threw it into the fire, and it all turned into gold, which was then sufficient for the ceremony. Later, when Wei fought against the Wu army, Yusheng warned him, saying, "Be careful not to put it in your mouth." Quanfeng did not understand this warning, and indeed, he was defeated at Xiangya Tan.

Tchen Shu, a Daoist priest from the Shan Tan Temple in Qi Prefecture, was said to be a hundred years old, but no one actually knew his age. He could walk hundreds of miles each day. When there were banquets in the prefecture, they would leave one seat empty in anticipation of his arrival, and he would always come from afar. Emperor Liang Wu heard of him and summoned him, but before the messenger arrived, he sighed and said, "I am old now. What benefit does it bring to the country to summon me unnecessarily?" A few days later, the messenger arrived. He was summoned again, but he did not go. During the Baodai era, he once burned incense in the courtyard at night, looked up to the sky and prayed, then turned back and wept bitterly. Soon after, there were military conflicts in the Huai region, and people believed he had foreseen this. Later, he crossed the river and lived in the abandoned Jingxing Temple in Yongxing, built a hut and lived alone, often having tigers and leopards following him, though few saw him. When he died, it took several days before his body was placed in the coffin, and his sweat had spread throughout the body. Sima Xiao, also known as Ning Zheng and Shou Zhong, traveled through the southern regions, often wearing simple clothes and walking barefoot, covering hundreds of miles each day. Whenever he visited various states upstream along the Yangtze River, he would stay at an inn for a long time. Before leaving, he told the innkeeper, "I have some bamboo utensils that I cannot take with me. Please burn them." The innkeeper said, "The wind is strong, and the bamboo house is low and narrow; it is not safe to light a fire." But Sima Xiao insisted. Soon, the fire grew strong, and flames shot out through the gaps in the bamboo roof, frightening everyone. After the fire was extinguished, all of Sima Xiao's possessions were destroyed, and his bed was scorched, but the mat under the bed was not burned. One day, he went to Hongzhou and took and ate salted fish. A child in the market shouted, "The Taoist priest is eating salted fish!" Sima Xiao became angry and hit the child, causing blood to flow from the child's forehead. The patrol arrested him and sent him to the military governor.

Jiao, whose name was long known, was skillfully persuaded by someone, but he then vehemently cursed them. The guard, overwhelmed with anger, beat him ten times. Jiao said to people, "If he beats me fifteen times, I can live for fifteen days; if he beats me ten, I will die in ten days." And indeed, this turned out to be true. He often stayed at a certain temple in She, suffering from severe dysentery. The temple master then verbally informed County Magistrate Yao Yun, who sent someone to visit him. Jiao said, "Why does the magistrate know of my illness?" The visitor told him. Jiao became angry, suddenly got dressed, and went straight into a certain mountain, very much disliking being talked about. After more than ten days, holding a large stick, he sought out the temple master, intending to beat him. The Taoist monks in the temple collectively bowed and begged for mercy, thus sparing him. He could also fake death, to the point of bruising, swelling, and rotting, and soon revived. Later, he entered Lu Mountain and stayed at the Simple and Quiet Temple, where he died after several days of drunkenness. At the moment of death, he commanded to place a stick in the coffin. Upon burial, the coffin was empty; when opened, only the stick was inside. Liu Tonggui My maternal grandfather, the Ai family, originally resided in Hongzhou. There was a person named Liu Tonggui who rented his house to live in, and the household consisted only of an old man and an old woman. He carried a basket of mushrooms to sell, drank, and returned home in the evening. After a long time, neighbors found it strange, and at night, dug a hole in the wall to spy on him. They saw him take out a clay pot and sprinkle water on it. In a moment, mushrooms grew, and by morning they were harvested. When he fell ill, he told the old woman, "When I die, definitely place a stick in the coffin." Upon his death, as he had said, the coffin was first lifted, and people felt it very heavy. By halfway, it gradually became lighter as if there were no body. When the coffin was shaken, only the stick was felt inside. When opened, only the stick was found.

### 史公镐 兵部尚书张翰在选拔官员时，有一位名叫史公镐的人，他是江南大将史公铢的弟弟。史公镐性格淡泊，喜欢讨论道义，曾请求担任扬子令，但因已任命了其他官员而未能如愿。张翰见他豁达且多才，便试探性地对他说：“暂且担任扬子尉，如何？”史公镐欣然同意。后来他担任了瑞昌令，在任上去世。当时天气晴朗，但他的住宅上却独自降下了云雨。有人看到云气中有一人，身穿红衣，骑着马，缓缓上升，直至极高的地方消失。 ### 董绍颜 在天佑年间，有一位名叫董绍颜的人，他擅长识人。何敬洙侍奉在李简身边时，董绍颜对他说：“此人非同寻常。”后来何敬洙多次被任命为节度使，成为当时著名的将领。起初，义祖镇守润州时，董绍颜也在那里，他经常视察衙门中的将领和军官，并对他们进行评价。有一位名叫蓝彦思的人对董绍颜说：“你说话很多，但有时也说得对。”董绍颜回答说：“你不要再说了，你不会有一个好结局。”蓝彦思说：“我作为军校，死于刀剑之下，这是我的职责，有什么可说的！”董绍颜说：“你宁愿死在刀剑之下吗？”后来，郡城多次遭遇灾害，衙门中也为此做了准备，有人制作了桶来储水，但士兵们因此手持桶刀作乱，蓝彦思在灾难中丧生。

Chapter 5 Mr. Geng Mr. Geng was the daughter of General Geng Qian from Jiangnan. She was young and wise, with good looks. She was literate and wrote a few poems, which often contained good meanings. She was knowledgeable in Taoist practices, able to control ghosts and demons, and skilled in alchemy, particularly the transmutation of base metals into gold and silver. She could perform strange and miraculous feats, which were extraordinary and vague, and nobody knew where her abilities came from. During the Bao Da period, the Jiang and Huai regions were prosperous, and the ruler liked elegant literature and enjoyed strange and wonderful things. He summoned her into the palace, observed her skills more closely, and did not treat her like ordinary court ladies, placing her in a separate courtyard and calling her Mr. Geng. Mr. Geng often wore a greenish-blue cloud robe and held a tablet when appearing before the ruler. She was distinguished and outstanding, with clear and fluent speech. Her hands were like bird claws, making them inconvenient to use, and she depended on others for food and drink. She also disliked walking in the palace and was often carried by others. She often wrote words and inscribed them on walls, calling herself North City Mr. Geng, and nobody knew the meaning of this title. Her skills were not always clearly displayed, but when needed, they would manifest silently, and the ruler valued her even more for this. When she first entered the palace, he asked her about alchemy and tested her, and everything she did was verified. She then expanded her experiments, making them simple and not complicated. Once, when the ruler was idly chatting with her, he said, "These are all made by fire. If you don't use fire, can they be done?" Mr. Geng replied, "Try it, perhaps it can be done." The ruler then took quicksilver, wrapped it in paper, and sealed it tightly. Mr. Geng placed it in her bosom, and after a long time, there was a sudden sound like tearing silk.

The scholar smiled and said, "Your Majesty, you once did not believe in my skills. Today, you can see them with your own eyes—how can you still doubt?" She handed the object to the emperor. The emperor looked at it, and the inscription remained as before. When he opened it, it had already turned into silver. Once, during a heavy snowfall, the emperor joked with her, saying, "Can you turn snow into silver, Master?" She replied, "Yes, it is possible." She then took some snow, packed it, and shaped it into the form of a silver ingot. She threw it into a blazing fire. Smoke and ash rose, and she gradually covered it with more charcoal. After a meal's time, she said, "It is done." Suddenly, it glowed bright red. She placed it on the ground, and when it cooled, it was a silver ingot, with the knife marks still visible. Looking at the bottom, it appeared as if melted butter and milk had dripped from it, having been melted by the fire. Because of this, the scholar created many silver ingots from snow. On the emperor's birthday, she often made utensils and presented them as birthday gifts. She was also very inventive, and her creations always surpassed those of others. Once, the South Sea offered rare items, including rose water and dragon brain essence. The rose water was fragrant and strong, and the dragon brain essence was beneficial to men. The emperor cherished these items and often mixed the dragon brain essence into wine to drink, its fragrance lingering in his mouth for several days. He also bestowed them upon his close ministers. When the scholar saw this, she said, "This is not the best." The emperor asked, "Can you make something better, Master?" She replied, "Let me try, and I can do it." She then took some dragon brain essence, wrapped it in fine silk, and hung it in a glass bottle. The emperor sealed and labeled the bottle himself, placing a cup of wine beside it to observe the process.

After a brief interval, the master said, "The dragon brain has become a paste." The emperor got up and listened closely, and indeed heard the sound of dripping liquid. He then drank again. Shortly after, he looked again and saw that the glass bottle contained only a ladleful of clear water. The next day, when he opened it, there was already half a bottle, and the fragrance was intense and pungent, far surpassing the old one. Later, the master became pregnant. One day, she told the emperor, "I will give birth tonight. A divine descendant, a sacred child, truly awaits here. Please provide the items needed for childbirth." The emperor arranged everything for her and ordered the palace attendants to stay in the room. At midnight, a violent storm with fierce winds and thunder struck, and everyone in the room was terrified. That night, she did not give birth. The next morning, the master's pregnancy had disappeared. The emperor was shocked and asked her, "Did you give birth in the midst of the lightning?" The master replied, "Yes, I gave birth in the midst of the lightning, and it was taken away by a divine being. I no longer have it." The master enjoyed drinking alcohol, and her sexual desires were roughly the same as those of ordinary people. She eventually died of illness. In ancient times, immortals often concealed their identities and blended into the world. Could she be such a person? I was recently in the south (Jiangnan) and often heard this story, but the hidden secrets of the palace have many differing accounts. Now that the south has been pacified, I am in the capital. I once visited Xu Shuijin, who is the grandson of Yi Zong, and he knows everything about the palace. He told me the full story. Mr. Geng, whose father was a high-ranking military officer, was a young female Taoist. She had a beautiful face and bird-like hands, often wore a green cloud robe, and called herself the Northern Great Master. She was first introduced through Song Qizhou.

Once I saw a palace maid holding the sweepings of feces, and she said to Emperor Yuanzong, "This substance is valuable; do not let it be discarded." She took it and placed it in a cauldron to cook and refine for a long time, and all of it turned into white gold. Once, when there was a snowstorm, she asked for a golden basin to hold snow, ordered the palace ladies to mold the snow into sticks, and threw them into the fire. After slowly lifting them out, all of them turned into white gold, with fingerprints still visible. She could also dry substances with fire, also known as frying and drying. Wheat grains turned into round pearls, shining brightly and dazzling the eyes. The country of the Great Food presented oil of borneol to Emperor Yuanzong, who secretly cherished it. Geng looked at it and said, "This is not good." He stored several pounds of white borneol in a silk bag and hung it up. After a while, the liquid flowed like a stream, and the fragrance surpassed what was offered. Thus, Geng gained favor with Emperor Yuanzong and became pregnant. On the eve of her delivery, there was a thunderstorm with lightning. When the storm cleared, the pregnancy was lost. After some time, the palace suddenly lost the whereabouts of Empress Dowager Song, and Geng also disappeared. For over a month, the inside and outside were greatly shocked. An informer said that she was at Fangshan Baohua Palace, thirty miles outside the capital, southeast of the city. Wu Ge the Immortal's residence has an elixir well, also known as Heavenly Seal Mountain, with a Baohua Palace stele. The palace base went through fire, right at the well's location, and the old people said that it was burned at that time. Emperor Yuanzong quickly ordered Prince Jingzhui of Qi to go and welcome the empress dowager. They found her with several Taoist priests who were drinking heavily, and they brought her back to the palace. The Taoist priests were all executed, and Geng could no longer enter the palace. However, he still traveled to Jianghuai, and later his fate was unknown. Nanjing's enthusiasts still have a true portrait of Mr. Geng.

According to the latter part of this biography, from Xu Shilang onwards, both Ma and Lu's *Nan Tang Shu* (History of the Southern Tang Dynasty) fully utilize it, except that the Northern Academy Master refers to the monk as Master Biqiu. It is unknown which version is correct. **Zhang Xun's Wife** Zhang Xun was a general under the Wu Emperor. He had a large mouth, and people at the time called him Zhang Da Kou (Big Mouth Zhang). Later, he achieved distinguished merit and served as the prefect of four prefectures (Hai, Mi, Huang, and Chang), the military governor of Chu Prefecture, and the deputy military governor of Huainan. He was posthumously bestowed the title of Taifu (Grand Tutor). His wife would often speak of matters in a supernatural manner. The Wu Emperor once bestowed armor and a horse upon Zhang Xun, but they were not as good as those of the other generals. The Wu Emperor dreamed of a woman wearing a pearl robe who told him, "The armor and horse you bestowed upon Zhang Xun are not good; they should be changed." The Wu Emperor asked Zhang Xun, "What kind of spirit is this?" Zhang Xun could not fathom it either. There was a clothing box that often opened and closed on its own, which Zhang Xun had never seen. One day, when his wife went out, Zhang Xun secretly looked inside and indeed saw a sword and a set of pearl robes. When his wife returned, she told Zhang Xun, "Did you open my clothing box?" Later, she became angry with Zhang Xun and left abruptly. Before this, his wife had given birth to a son who was still being breastfed. Zhang Xun pitied the child's separation from his mother, so that night, he caressed and pitied the child and lay close to him. By midnight, his wife suddenly entered the bed from outside, intending to nurse her son. Zhang Xun scolded her, saying, "If you've left, why have you come back?" His wife did not respond and left suddenly. Gradually, Zhang Xun realized that there seemed to be something dirty and wet in the bedding. He got up, lit a candle, and looked, only to find that his son's head was already gone. Finally, no one knew where it had gone.

Zhang Biao A recluse scholar named Zhang Biao from Fujian was known for his magical abilities and could communicate with the underworld. Sometimes, he would sleep for three or five days as if dead, yet his body remained warm. After waking up, he often spoke about matters from the underworld. He sometimes foretold the future, and all of his predictions came true. The people in the region greatly trusted him. Wang Bao Yi, at the end of the Tang dynasty, served as the governor of Fujian. He carried a document to the imperial court, but the roads were impassable, so he had to sail the sea, and he drowned as a result. His grandson, Kan, remained in Fujian. When a family member fell ill, he asked Zhang Biao to pray to the underworld on their behalf. Zhang Biao agreed and said, "I saw your ancestor, who has a position in the underworld." He described the family affairs in detail, and everything he said was true.

Master Yu Master Yu lived in the West Mountain of Hongzhou. He often held flowers throughout the four seasons and did not wish to be close to people. Once he arrived at the Yingsheng Palace and placed flowers before a Daoist statue. A Daoist priest prepared tea, placed it on a food table, and waited for people to leave. Then Master Yu drank the tea. After finishing, he placed the tea cup on the table, performed a long bow, and left. If someone bowed to him, he would also bow back, but he did not speak with people. Scholar Jiang Scholar Jiang from Shezhou had a calm and quiet nature, loved the Dao, and could control spirits and ghosts. In the village, there was a woman possessed by a ghost, and family members occasionally saw it. One night, her husband felt someone sleeping with his wife, so he rushed up and grabbed it, calling for someone to bring fire to tie it up. When the fire arrived, they only saw the belt tied around themselves. They sought widely for talismans and exorcisms, but ultimately could not eliminate it. Then they went to see Scholar Jiang, who said, "I can control it, but I do not wish to make enemies with the ghosts. Once told me, I will send it away kindly." He instructed him to return home and sweep a room clean, and to have a young boy brew tea and wait. When I arrive, do not let anyone peek. As he said, Scholar Jiang soon arrived, entered the room, and sat. He instructed the young boy to greet the guest. Indeed, they saw a green-clad young man, whose appearance was very dignified. He invited him into the room, and the young man bowed twice to Scholar Jiang. Scholar Jiang ordered him to sit, and he sat. They drank tea without exchanging a word, and the young man bowed twice and left. From then on, the woman returned to her normal state.

Once upon a time, there was a man who went into the mountains to cut down trees. He was possessed by a spirit, who spoke to him, saying, "This tree is my dwelling place. You are now felling it. Where will I rely on? I will borrow your body as my lair." From then on, the man felt something moving inside his skin, from head to toe, going everywhere. He could not bear the suffering. He went to see Jiang. Before he arrived, the spirit had already gone to Jiang's residence. Jiang lived in a house with a tower, and there were dense bamboo groves to the north of the tower. Jiang was sitting on the upper floor of the tower and felt the spirit was in the bamboo grove. He called out to ask it, and the spirit told him everything, and requested forgiveness. Jiang said, "I already know." Soon after, the man arrived. Jiang told him, "You can find an unoccupied house in the village, and come back and tell me." The man went to the village and found such a house. Jiang gave him a small piece of paper with a name written on it, and warned him, saying, "When you reach the empty house, throw it away." As instructed, when he arrived at the empty house, he discarded the paper, and the sick person recovered. Once there was a person troubled by a qiu ghost. His family placed paintings on the upper floor of the house, and all were defiled by impure substances. They told Jiang about it. Jiang said, "Just close the door of the tower for three days, and it will make it go away." As he said, after three days, they opened it, and all the impurities were gone, and the paintings were as before. I know someone who has lived in Shezhou all his life and has personally seen these events.

Qian the Scholar Qian the Scholar, at the end of the Tianyou era, traveled through the Jianghuai region and once stayed at the home of Yang in Jinling. Initially, the Wu Kingdom established Jinling as a prefecture, building walls that extended west to the river and east to the tidal channel. Qian pointed out a desolate area to the west of the city and advised Yang to buy it. Yang followed his advice. When the area was later developed into a bustling capital, the land Yang had purchased became a prosperous location, and he built a multi-story building to serve as a tavern. The Scholar often stayed at Yang's home, and one night he suddenly woke up, telling others, "There is a commotion of troops in the underworld, saying they are connected to a high-ranking official, disturbing me and preventing me from sleeping." No one could understand this. The next day, Yi Zong arrived in Jinling from Jingkou, and at the time, no one had foreseen this. He once saw a person and told them, "Your divine punishment is imminent; you should quickly make an apology and take responsibility." The person replied, "I have not realized I have done anything wrong." Qian said, "You should deeply reflect on this."

After a long silence, the man said, "Yesterday, my meal was unsatisfactory, and in anger I discarded the food into the ditch." Qian replied, "Exactly. You may quickly retrieve the discarded food." The man did so, intending to wash away its filth with water, but soon a great thunderclap and lightning struck. Qian said, "Hasten to eat the filthy food." As he did so, the thunder and lightning ceased. Once, someone drew a depiction of Qian, and when Qian saw it, he said, "I am actually less than this one who is constantly in the presence of sages." The man did not understand. Later, a monk took the image and placed it inside the Zhigong Tower, and people believed this act was appropriate. Later, the revered founder took the image into the palace and displayed it in the inner chamber. Qian also often made prophecies, speaking of matters in the East, saying that the Li family's mandate was approximately double that of the Yang family. Initially, Wu completely controlled the Jianghuai region, a total of forty-six years, while the Li family ruled for thirty-nine years. Some said that the Yang family held the imperial title for twenty-five years until the abdication, hence the Li family's reign was approximately double.

The Recluse of Runzhou The Recluse of Runzhou, whose name is unknown, was a man of high moral character and possessed mystical knowledge. Everyone respected and trusted him. During the rebellion of Anrenyi, the people of the prefecture were terrified and all wanted to flee in panic. Someone said, "The recluse remains calm here, unharmed." Thus, the people gradually calmed down. A relative of the recluse took his family out of the prefecture to avoid the danger. The recluse had a daughter who had already married and could not accompany them. She entrusted herself to the recluse, and he agreed. Soon, the city was under siege. The recluse told the woman, "Take an item from your home and bring it to me. I will help you escape the danger." The woman then took a knife from her home and brought it to him. The recluse held the knife and used his hands to fold and press it. He then gave it back to her, saying, "Hold this as if it were a dignified and simple object. Wait for the city to send out troops and follow them out. You can avoid harm." Following his instructions, she was in the midst of the crowd, and no one saw her. She reached a village inn dozens of miles outside the city and saw her brother in front of her, but her brother did not see her. She then threw the knife into the water and went back. Her brother then saw her and was surprised, asking, "How did you get here?" The woman told him everything. Her brother then told her to take the knife and hold it again, but she could no longer hide her form. After the city fell, the recluse disappeared without a trace.

A Strange Person in Jiankang Outside the eastern suburbs and altar gate of Jiankang's city walls, there once lived a man whose name was never mentioned. He built a small house near the wild waters to the north, just enough to shelter himself. Inside the house, there were only a few household items, and nothing else. Every day, he would enter the city, claiming to be a beggar, but he never went through the streets or markets. Instead, he would wander leisurely around the temples. People knew about him. A patrol official reported this to the emperor, who ordered an investigation into the man's origins and asked what he desired. When questioned, the man had no specific requests. During the severe winter, the government distributed winter coats to the poor. Seeing that the man was poorly clothed, they gave him one, but he refused it. When they insisted, he gave the coat to someone else. People found this even more strange, so they followed him, asking him to move his residence and observe where he went. He then demolished his house and moved to the southwest of Yuanwu Lake, into the orchard of the eunuch Zhang Qi. The orchard was overgrown with weeds and also had wild waters. He built another small house by the water to live in. It snowed heavily for several days, and the orchard workers did not see him come and go, thinking he had frozen to death. They searched the house and found it in ruins, saying, "He must have died." They reported this to the authorities. Later, they opened the house and saw that he was sound asleep in the snow. He startled awake, showing no signs of being affected by the cold, and then left. Afterward, no one knew where he went.

A Scholar from Hongzhou Cheng Youwen served as the Recorder and Assistant Military Officer of Hongzhou. His residence was located near a busy thoroughfare and had a window. One day, sitting by the window, he saw that the rain had just stopped, leaving the path muddy with only a faint trail. He noticed a poor child selling shoes, whose appearance was very destitute. Suddenly, a young man of bad character met the child, and the child's shoe got caught and fell into the mud. The child cried out for the price of the shoe, but the young man scolded him and refused to pay. The child said, "My family has no food for the morning, and we were going to sell the shoe to buy food, but it has all been ruined." A bookworm passed by and felt compassion for the child, offering to pay for the shoe. The young man angrily said, "The child came to me for money, what business is it of yours?" He then insulted the bookworm. The bookworm showed a look of displeasure. Cheng admired the bookworm's kindness, called him over to talk, and was greatly impressed by him. He invited the bookworm to stay for the night and they talked late into the evening. Cheng temporarily went inside, and when he came back out, the bookworm had disappeared. All the doors were locked, and Cheng could not find him. A short while later, the bookworm reappeared and said, "This morning, that young man of bad character, I could not tolerate him, so I cut off his head." He then threw the head to the ground. Cheng was shocked and said, "This person truly offended a gentleman, but cutting off someone's head and having blood on the ground, won't that bring trouble?" The bookworm replied, "No trouble." He then took out a little medicine and applied it to his head, grasped his hair and rubbed it, and it all turned into water. He then told Cheng, "I have no way to repay you, but I wish to teach you this skill." Cheng said, "I am not a person from the otherworld, I dare not accept your teachings." The bookworm then performed a long bow and left, and all the doors were locked, and he disappeared without a trace.

An Old Man from Hangzhou When Zhong Chuan was stationed in Hongzhou, he once sent an officer named Yan to the central region of Zhejiang. Yan arrived in Hangzhou during the Cold Food Festival, when people from the city were out of town, and the streets were crowded with men and women. Yan also went out. He saw an old man and an old woman drinking together in the fields. Suddenly, the old man ascended into the sky, riding a cloud, and the crowd roared with excitement. The old woman looked up and cried out in sorrow. The old man descended several dozen feet and comforted her with his hand. Shortly after, he ascended again, rising to great heights and disappearing. My maternal grandfather, Ai, knew Yan personally and heard this story from him. A Fisherman from Sintan Wu Zhi, the founder of the Wu Kingdom, was an official responsible for inspecting the eight camps in Lu Prefecture. He traveled to Sintan and rested by the riverside. A fisherman rowed his boat up to him and gave him several fish, saying, "The descendants of Prince Wu will rise to power in succession." He then pointed around and said, "All these are your mountains and rivers." Wu Zhi found this strange and intended to give him something, but the fisherman ignored the gift and left.

**Soldiers of Xuanzhou** The son of the Yi Zu, Prince Wei Zhizheng, was stationed in Xuanzhou. There was a soldier whose name was unknown; his family consisted only of himself and his wife. One day, the husband returned from outside, sought water to bathe, changed into new clothes, and sat on a rope bed until he died. His wife saw this and was greatly shocked, saying, "You are dead." She did not weep, but bathed and changed clothes as well, and sat facing her husband until she died too. Prince Wei therefore buried them together in a single grave. *Jianghuai Strange People* [Song dynasty] Wu Shu {Zhengtong Daozang edition} **Master Sijiao** Master Sijiao, also known as Ningzheng and also known as Shouzhong, wandered in the southern provinces. He often wore a cap and a coarse robe, and walked in wooden clogs, traveling up to a thousand or a hundred miles in a single day. His coarse robe never changed, yet it remained always new. His actions were rough and violent, and no one dared to approach him. He was able to feign death, until his body became bruised, swollen, and rotten, and then, after a short while, he revived. Once, he stayed at the Kaiyuan Temple in Xuanzhou, and traveled from Xuanzhou to She County. At that time, the Daoist Master Shaoxiu Mo was also going to She County. At the city gate, he met Master Sijiao and traveled with him. Shaoxiu Mo avoided him and went ahead to a garrison town, where he rested at an inn. Later, Sijiao arrived, and Shaoxiu Mo hid and secretly observed him. He saw Sijiao enter a different inn, summon the innkeeper, and drink with him, then insulted him. At first, the innkeeper respectfully thanked him, but Sijiao did not stop, and struck him again. Soon after, they fought each other. Sijiao suddenly fell to the ground; when they looked at him, he was already dead, his body cold and his color changed. The whole town gathered to watch. They then assembled the villagers, bound the innkeeper, examined the body, and questioned him, intending to send him to the state. It was already late in the evening, and they planned to depart the next morning. By midnight, they heard from the inn again: "Master Sijiao has already disappeared!"

People only realized that 郊 was pretending to be dead and released his master. The next day at dawn, 修默 set out, traveling about a hundred miles ahead, and asked someone, "When did Master 司马 pass by here?" The person replied, "He passed by this morning already." The next day, he traveled another hundred miles and asked again, "He passed by early yesterday." When he arrived in Shezhou, the same was said. Whenever he traveled to the various states upstream, he would stay at an inn for a long time. About to leave, he told his host, "I cannot take all my bamboo utensils with me, so I will burn them." The host said, "The wind is high, and the bamboo house is low and narrow, so it is unsafe to light a fire." But 郊 persisted, and everyone bowed and tried to persuade him. 郊 became angry and refused to listen. He then lit a fire inside the room, holding a large stick and standing by the door, hitting anyone who dared to approach. 郊 was strong, and no one dared to come near him. Soon the fire grew intense, and flames emerged from the gaps in the bamboo roof tiles, causing everyone to be terrified. Afterward, the fire went out, and all of 郊's utensils were destroyed, but the bed he lay on was heavily burned, and his bedding remained unburned. There was a person named 朱翱, who was a legal official in Chizhou.

Xie visited Zhu and said to him, "Your complexion looks very bad; you will fall ill. I will leave now. If you can think of me while you are sick, or call my name, there will be a response." Zhu did not believe him. After more than ten days, Zhu indeed fell ill with a fever, and for several days, it became severe. Suddenly remembering what Xie had said, Zhu thought it was very mysterious and therefore bowed his head in prayer and sought protection. Initially, Zhu was already feeling unwell and saw someone in front of him. There was a minor official named Chen who usually followed his orders and was ordered to enter the room to attend to his illness. Zhu also ordered him to leave. Family members guarded him, and no one was allowed to enter. By then, Zhu saw Chen holding a bowl of medicine and bringing it to him. Zhu drank it and immediately felt better both mentally and physically. He called out to his family members, saying, "Just now, the medicine Chen brought was very effective. Should be given another dose." The family members were surprised and said, "We never let anyone into the room. How could Chen be here?" Zhu then realized that Xie had been protecting him. From then on, Zhu's illness gradually improved. Xie once lived in a certain temple in Shezhou and suffered from severe dysentery. The head of the temple wanted to report to the authorities but first informed Xie.

Jiao angrily said, "My illness is just recovering, why bother so much?" Having gradually become seriously ill, the abbot had no choice but to speak to County Magistrate Yao Yun, who then sent someone to inquire about him. Jiao asked, "Why does County Magistrate Yao know about my illness?" The messenger informed him, and Jiao, in a rage, suddenly got up and prepared, directly entering a certain mountain, his movements like flying. After more than ten days, holding a large stick, he sought the abbot, intending to beat him. The Taoist monks in the temple collectively worshipped and begged for help, and he was thus spared. Once, he went to Hongzhou City, tried some pickled fish, and ate it. A child in the market shouted, "The Taoist monk is eating pickled fish!" Jiao, enraged, struck the child with an object, causing the child to bleed. The patrol officer arrested Jiao, sent him to the guard commander, who knew his name, and then kindly persuaded him. Jiao then loudly cursed and raged, and the guard commander, unable to bear his anger, beat him with a stick ten times. Jiao said to others, "If he beats me fifteen times, I can live for fifteen days; if he beats me ten times, I will die in ten days." And indeed, it turned out to be true. Later, he entered Mount Lu, lived in Jianji Temple, became drunk and lay down, dying after several days. On his deathbed, he ordered a stick to be placed in the coffin. When the coffin was buried, it was found to be empty. Upon opening it, only the stick was there.

**Mr. Qian** Mr. Qian, during the end of the Tianyou era, traveled through the Jianghuai region and once stayed at the home of Yang in Jinling. Initially, the Wu Kingdom had established Jinling as a state capital and built a city west of the Pao River and east to the Chao Creek. Qian pointed out a piece of land about a mile west of the city, which was overgrown and neglected, and advised Yang to buy it. Yang followed his advice. Later, when the area was developed into a bustling city, the land that Yang had purchased happened to be in a place of great activity and importance, and he built a multi-story building as an inn there. One night, Qian suddenly woke up and told others, "There are noisy troops underground, claiming to be connected to the '令公,' disturbing me so much that I cannot sleep." No one could understand this. The next day, Wang Yan, the founder of the Southern Tang Dynasty, arrived in Jinling from Zhenjiang, and no one had predicted this. Qian saw a person and said to him, "You are facing divine punishment, and you should quickly apologize and take responsibility." The person replied, "I do not understand having done anything wrong." Qian said, "You should deeply reflect on it."

After a long time, the person said, "Yesterday, I was dissatisfied with the food and became angry with my subordinates, so I threw the food into the ditch." Money said, "That is exactly it. You can quickly take the discarded food." He took it and was about to wash it with water to remove the impurity, when soon after, there was a great thunder and lightning. Money said, "Quickly take and eat the impure food." He did so, and the thunder and lightning ceased. Once, someone drew a picture of Money, and when Money saw it, he said, "I am actually not as good as this one who is always in the presence of the sage." The person did not understand. Later, a monk took the picture and placed it in the Zhi Gong Pagoda, and people believed it was appropriate. Later, the founding emperor took it back into the palace and placed it in the inner chambers. Money also often spoke in cryptic language, predicting future events. He said about the fate of the Li dynasty, "In a vague way, it doubles Yang." Initially, the Wu family controlled the Jiang region for 46 years, while the Li dynasty lasted 39 years. Some say that the Yang family claimed high status and the transition of power lasted 20 years, so it is only vaguely doubled. Now, let's do some post-drafting research on the final translation. During this phase, thorough research is essential to address components of the final translation that pose translation challenges. The goal is to establish a comprehensive translation plan that covers the following: * Idiomatic Expressions: Identify idiomatic or archaic expressions that cannot be directly translated word-for-word into English. * Cultural and Historical Allusions: Identify references to historical events, figures, or cultural practices that require contextual explanation or adaptation. * Syntactic Ambiguities: Identify grammatically ambiguous constructions or elliptical passages unique to Classical Chinese syntax. * Proper Nouns: Identify people, places, titles, and canonical terms and determine how to render them consistently.

聂师道，是歙州人。他年轻时就对道家学说感兴趣。唐朝末年，于涛担任歙州刺史，他的哥哥方外是道士，住在郡城南面的山中，聂师道经常拜访他。于涛有时会去拜访方外，讨论郡中的政务，都会向他咨询，因此将这座山命名为问政山。吴朝因为聂师道曾经居住在这座山上，所以称他为问政先生。起初，方外住在山中时，郡中的人很少信奉他。等到聂师道到来后，每天前来的人越来越多，这个地方变得富裕繁荣。聂师道曾经和一位朋友一起旅行，到了一家旅店，朋友得了重病，村里没有医生。有人告诉病人说：“如果能吃一点不干净的食物，就可以治好。”等到病情危急时，又劝他这样做。病人面有难色，聂师道劝他说：“情况紧急了，何必在这里犹豫，我先尝尝。”于是他取来吃了，病人被他的诚意所感动，也吃了一些，结果病果然痊愈了。后来，给事中裴枢担任歙州刺史，正值唐朝末年，朝廷的诏令无法传达。宣州的田頵和池州的陶雅举兵反叛，围攻歙州数月，歙州人多次击退他们。后来城中粮食耗尽，援军断绝，有人提议投降，但城中已经杀死了许多外来的军队，没有人敢出城去谈判。聂师道于是自告奋勇前去，裴枢说：“你是个道士，怎么能去兵荒马乱的地方呢？还是换上衣服再去吧。”聂师道回答说：“我已经接受了道法的教诲，不容易改变装束。”于是他被缒下城去。两位将领起初非常惊讶，但与他交谈后，非常高兴地说：“真是个真正的道人。”誓约达成后，又派人将他送回城中。

By the appointed time, the general had not yet finished and wanted to extend the deadline, sending Teacher Dao out to explain it further. People thought it was inconsistent and considered it dangerous, but Teacher Dao showed no sign of difficulty. When he met with the two generals again, both said, "There is no problem, just follow your instructions." At that time, the people in the city were saved, due to Teacher Dao's efforts. General Wu, the founder of the Wu state, heard of his reputation and summoned him to Guangling, where he built the Purple Extreme Palace for him to reside in. One night, a group of bandits entered where he was staying and took everything, even the utensils. Teacher Dao said to them, "You are thieves, taking my possessions to relieve hunger and cold. What will you do with these things?" He then led them to a secluded room, gave them all the gold and silk, and told them, "You should leave from there, no one will be patrolling, so you can avoid trouble." The bandits followed his advice and escaped unharmed.

After the Wu court sent Shidao to Longhu Mountain to perform a ritual, he encountered a group of bandits on the road who robbed him and were about to harm him. One of them looked at Shidao carefully and said to his companions, "Do not harm the master." He ordered them to return everything they had taken, and the bandits followed his instructions. He then said to Shidao, "I am the same person who was a bandit in the Purple Extreme Palace in Yangzhou years ago. Grateful for the master's great benevolence, I am now repaying you." Shidao later died in Guangling. At the time, an envoy was sent to Hu-Xiang. When the envoy returned, he arrived at a certain place and saw Shidao. He asked, "Why are you here?" Shidao replied, "The court sent me to perform a ritual at Nanyue." The envoy thought this was true. However, when he entered the Wu territory, he learned that Shidao had already died. Shidao's nephew-grandson, Shao Yuan, was young and entered the Taoist path. He had a harmonious and elegant appearance and demeanor, and he was skilled in writing. He died in his twenties. At first, Shao Yuan was already very ill. Four cranes gathered on the roof where he lived. When he died, people saw five cranes fly into the sky and fly away.

Yu Da Yu Da lived in the western mountains of Hongzhou, where he was often seen holding flowers, seemingly indifferent to the changing seasons and uninterested in human company. Once, he visited the Temple of the Holy Emperor, placing flowers before a Daoist statue. A Daoist prepared tea and set it on a food table. When the others left, Yu took the tea, drank it, placed the cup back on the table, and bowed deeply before departing. If anyone bowed to him, he would bow back, but he did not speak to people. There was a young man who was interested in Daoism and wished to follow Yu, but he could not succeed. One day, the young man bowed and said, "I wish to serve you, Master." Yu walked away without looking back. The young man followed, holding his clothes, but Yu drove him away, yet he would not leave. They climbed mountains and crossed rivers, never letting go. They arrived at a place by the water and sat down. Yu asked the young man, "Are you thirsty?" The young man replied, "Yes." Yu took out something like tea powder from his chest and gave it to him, saying, "Put this in your mouth and drink some water." The young man followed his instructions. After a while, he fell asleep, and when he woke up, Yu had disappeared.

Li Mengfu Li Mengfu was a man who often roamed the streets of Hongzhou, about twenty years old, short and fair-skinned, handsome and graceful like a jade figure. He lived a life of unrestrained indulgence, always adorned with flowers in the four seasons, and frequented the city's taverns, singing loudly and getting drunk. Those who enjoyed his company often invited him to drink. Sometimes they asked him to compose lyrics, and he would do so immediately, without much thought, but each piece had its own charm. When Zhong Chuan, the military governor of Hongzhou, heard of his reckless and delusional behavior, he intended to punish him. While in prison, Mengfu offered more than ten poems, one of which read: "Adorning with flowers and drinking do no harm, fishing songs and fishing tunes do not hinder time." Zhong Chuan, however, did not punish him. Later, Governor Li Qiong of Guilin sent an envoy to Hongzhou, stating that Mengfu was his younger brother, and asked for his release. Zhong Chuan ordered a search in the city's inns, but people said, "Mengfu did not return." From then on, his whereabouts remained unknown.

#### Liu Tonggui Liu Tonggui lived in a house belonging to the Ai family in Hongzhou. He rented the house from them, and the household consisted only of his elderly parents. Every morning, he would carry a basket of bamboo mats to sell, and every evening, he would return home drunk. After a long time, the neighbors found his behavior strange and, at night, dug a hole in the wall to spy on him. They saw him take out a jar of soil and spit water on it, and in a short while, mushrooms grew. He harvested them until dawn. Later, when the husband fell ill, he told his wife, "When I die, make sure to place a staff in my coffin." When he passed away, she followed his instructions. At first, when they lifted the coffin, people felt it was heavy, but by the halfway point, it gradually became as light as if it were empty. The coffin drifted away, and they only felt a staff inside. When they opened it, they only found the staff. #### Mr. Geng Mr. Geng was the daughter of a military officer named Geng Qian from the Jiang and Huai regions. She was young, bright, and wise, with an attractive appearance. She enjoyed reading and wrote some poetry, which often had good meaning. She was also proficient in esoteric arts and could control spirits and ghosts. She was skilled in alchemy and transforming strange things, and her abilities were wonderful and grand, though no one knew how she acquired them. During the Bao Da era, the Jiang and Huai regions were prosperous and flourishing. The emperor, who liked elegant literature and enjoyed strange and wonderful things, summoned her into the palace to observe her skills more closely. Instead of treating her as part of the official group, he placed her in a separate pavilion and called her Mr. Geng. Mr. Geng often wore a greenish-yellow cloak. When she saw the emperor, she often carried a tablet and was dazzling and extraordinary. Her words were clear and fluent. Her hands were like bird claws, making them inconvenient for use, and she relied on others for her meals. She also did not like walking around the palace and was often carried by someone. She would write poetry and inscribe it on the walls, calling herself "Mr. Geng of the North," though no one knew the meaning of this title.

The master's skills are not always openly displayed but are revealed when needed. When faced with an event, they respond, and their abilities become apparent in a subtle way. The emperor values him even more because of this. When he first entered the palace, the emperor asked him about alchemy (turning base metals into gold or silver), and all the experiments were successful. He continued to perform these experiments, and they were simple and not complicated. The emperor once, during a leisurely moment, asked the master, "These transformations are all achieved through fire. If no fire is needed, can they still be done?" The master replied, "Let me try, and it should be possible." The emperor then took mercury, wrapped it in paper, and sealed it tightly. The master placed it in his robe. After a long while, there was a sudden sound like tearing silk. The master smiled and said, "Your Majesty often did not believe in my methods, but today you can see it with your own eyes. Can you still not believe?" He handed it to the emperor, who examined it and found the seal intact. When he opened it, it had already turned into silver. Once during a heavy snowfall, the emperor joked with him, saying, "Can you turn snow into silver?" The master replied, "Yes, it can be done." He took some snow, shaped it into the form of a silver ingot, and threw it into the blazing fire. Dust and ash rose up, and he slowly covered it with more coal. After a while, he said, "It’s done."

The master then carried it out, burning bright and deeply red, and placed it on the ground. When it cooled, it shone lustrously as a bar of silver, with the marks of the knife still visible. Looking down, it appeared as if it were dripping like milk, for it had initially been melted and dissolved by fire. Because of this, the master produced a great deal of snow silver. On the emperor's birthday, he made utensils every time and offered them as a birthday gift. He also had many clever ideas, and his works were always superior to others. Once, the southern sea presented rare objects as tribute, including rosewater and dragon brain tincture. The rosewater was clear, fragrant, and strongly aromatic, while the dragon brain tincture was beneficial for men. The emperor treasured them, often mixing the dragon brain tincture with wine and consuming it. The fragrance lingered in his mouth for days, and he also bestowed it upon his close officials. The master said, "This is not the best." The emperor asked, "Can you make it?" The master replied, "Try it, and it should be possible." He took the dragon brain, wrapped it in fine silk, and hung it in a glass bottle. The emperor personally sealed and labeled it, placing wine beside it and watching it. After a meal, the master said, "The dragon brain has turned into a liquid." The emperor got up himself, leaned in to listen, and indeed heard the sound of dripping. And he continued drinking.

In a short while, upon looking again, he saw that the glass bottle contained a clear liquid like water from a ladle. The next day, when he opened it, it was already half full, and the fragrance was intense and pungent, far surpassing the old one. Later, the master became pregnant. One day, she told the emperor, "Tonight I will give birth. A divine descendant, a sacred child, truly lies within me. Please prepare all the items needed for childbirth." The emperor arranged everything, and he also ordered palace attendants to stay in the room. At midnight, there was a violent wind and thunder, and everyone in the room was terrified. That night, she did not give birth again. The next morning, the master's belly had returned to its normal size. The emperor was surprised and asked her about it. She replied, "Last night, my son was born amidst lightning, and a divine being took him away; I could not get him back." The master enjoyed drinking alcohol, and in terms of sexual desire, she was similar to ordinary people. Later, she eventually died of illness. In ancient times, immortals often concealed their true nature and blended with the common people. Could the master be one of them? Recently, while in the south, I often heard this story, but the secrets of the palace are full of mysteries, and there are many differing accounts. After the south was pacified, when I was in the capital, I once visited Xu Shu, who was the grandson of Yi Zu. He knew all the affairs of the palace. I inquired about the matter, and he provided a detailed account to me.

Pan Yi Pan Yi was the son of Pan Peng, a Censorial Magistrate of Dali. When he was young, he lived in Hezhou, where he chopped wood and gathered firewood on Jilong Mountain to support his parents. Once, he crossed the river to Jinling and moored his boat at the mouth of Qinhuai River. An old man asked to be taken across the river together, and Pan Yi, respecting his age, agreed. It was snowing heavily, and Pan Yi bought wine to drink with him. When they were in the middle of the river, the wine had run out, and Pan Yi regretted having so little, not being able to get drunk. The old man said, "I also have wine." He then unbuckled his hat, took out a small gourd from his hair, and poured it. They drank endlessly without running out. Pan Yi was amazed and became even more respectful. When they reached the shore, the old man said to Pan Yi, "You are filial to your parents and have a Daoist aura; you can be taught." He then taught him Daoist techniques. From then on, Pan Yi did strange and mysterious things, and the world called him "Pan the Immortal." He could hold mercury in his hand, which turned into silver when touched. Once, he entered a household and saw many fallen leaves in the pond. He said to the owner, "This can be used for a game." He ordered to scoop them up with something and placed them on the ground. According to the size of the leaves, they all turned into fish. Then he threw them back into the water, and the leaves returned to their original state.

There was a man named Kui Liang who often visited his close relative's home. Several people were sitting together when they saw Yi passing by the gate. The host summoned him, and he came. He then said to Yi, "Please show us a trick to entertain us." Yi replied, "I can do that." He looked around and saw an iron anvil at the door. He told the host, "If I can get this iron anvil, it can be used for entertainment." The host lent it to him. When he got it, Kui Liang took out a small knife and cut it finely until it was all gone. The guests were shocked. He then said, "The borrowed items should not be damaged." He put it back together, and the anvil returned to its original state. He then took out an old square handkerchief and told the people, "Do not underestimate this; if someone else is in urgent need, you should not borrow it from me, because others cannot obtain it." He raised it to cover his face and stepped back a few steps, and it was no longer visible. He could recite books he had never seen before. Sometimes he would roll it up and seal it, place it in front of him, and then recite the entire book starting with the first word. He could recognize all the changes, additions, and corrections made in the text. He did many such things, but they are not all recorded here, and later he died of illness.

A Recluse Scholar of Ruzhou A recluse scholar of Ruzhou, whose name is unknown, was a noble and knowledgeable man who possessed spiritual wisdom. People all respected and trusted him. During the rebellion known as the "An Renyi Uprising," the residents of the prefecture were filled with fear and panic, and many wished to flee. Someone said, "The recluse scholar remains calm here; he will surely be unharmed." Thus, the people began to feel somewhat reassured. The recluse scholar had a relative who took their family out of the prefecture to escape the turmoil. The recluse scholar had a daughter who had already married and could not accompany her family. She entrusted herself to the recluse scholar, who agreed to take care of her. Soon after, the situation became urgent, and the recluse scholar told his daughter, "Take one item from your home and come to me; I will ensure your safety." The daughter then took a knife from her home and went to the recluse scholar. He pressed the knife down with his hand and gave it back to her, saying, "Hold this knife as if it were a formal document. Wait for the troops to leave the city, and follow them out; you will be safe." Following his instructions, she moved through the crowd unnoticed. She eventually reached a village several miles outside the city, where she saw her brother. When she approached him, he did not see her. She then discarded the knife into the water and returned. Her brother finally saw her and exclaimed, "How did you get here?" The daughter explained everything in detail. Her brother then instructed her to take the knife again, but this time it could no longer conceal her form. Later, when the city fell, the recluse scholar's whereabouts became unknown.

**Military Officials of Hongzhou** When Zhong Chuan was stationed as the governor of Hongzhou, he once sent an officer named Yan to the region of Zhejiang. Upon arriving in Hangzhou, Yan found it was the Cold Food Festival, and the city was bustling with people, men and women crowding the streets. Yan also went out to observe, and he saw an elderly couple drinking together in the wild. Suddenly, the old man ascended into the clouds, and the crowd shouted in excitement. The old woman looked up and wept bitterly. The old man descended several dozen feet and reassured her with his hand. Soon, he ascended again, reaching a great height and disappearing. In Hongzhou, the Ai family, who had known Yan from before, heard the story directly from him. **Shi Gong Hao** Shi Gong Hao was the younger brother of Shi Gong Zhu, a prominent general in southern Jiangnan. He had a calm and simple disposition, enjoying the pursuit of the Way, and he never showed joy or anger. When others disturbed him, he did not take it to heart. After gaining prominence, he dressed in fresh and elegant attire, and whenever he visited someone’s home, he always took off his clothes before sitting down, not paying attention to whether he was a guest or host. On his way out, he mistakenly wore someone else’s old and tattered clothes, but he did not realize it. Someone held his clothes and followed him, and he only realized it and then changed them. The Minister of War Zhang Han was in charge of the personnel department, and Shi Gong Hao sought to be the magistrate of Yangzi. However, it was already too late, and he did not succeed. Zhang Han, seeing his open-minded and unrestrained nature, believed he was versatile and adaptable, and said to him, "How about becoming the deputy magistrate of Yangzi?" Shi Gong Hao gladly accepted. Later, he became the magistrate of Ruichang and died in office. At the time, it was clear and sunny, but there was a cloud and rain above his residence. Some people claimed to have seen a man in red clothes riding a horse, slowly ascending to a great height and disappearing.

Jiang the Hermit In the prefecture of She, there was a hermit named Jiang, whose nature was calm and secluded, and who had a fondness for the Dao. He was able to subdue ghosts and demons. Once, there was a woman in his village who was possessed by a ghost, and her family members occasionally saw it. One night, her husband woke up to find someone sleeping with his wife, and he quickly got up and held onto her, calling others to bring a light so they could bind her. When the light arrived, they saw that it was actually their own wife, holding onto her own waistband. They widely sought talismans and exorcisms, but finally could not eliminate the ghost, so they went to see Jiang. Jiang said, "Although I am capable of driving it away, I do not wish to make enemies with ghosts and gods. Since you have told me about it, I will send it away." He instructed them to return home, sweep and clean a room, and have a young servant boil tea, waiting for him to arrive, and not to let anyone peek in. Following his instructions, Jiang soon arrived, entered the room and sat down, and had the young servant go out to welcome the guests. Indeed, a young man in green clothing appeared, with a very dignified appearance. He was invited into the room, saw Jiang, and bowed twice. Jiang motioned for him to sit, and he did so. They sipped tea, without exchanging a single word, and he bowed twice before leaving. From then on, the woman returned to normal.

A man went into the mountains to cut wood and was possessed by a ghost. The ghost spoke to him, saying, "This tree is my dwelling place, and now you are cutting it down. Where will I find shelter? I must use your body as my dwelling." From that moment, the man felt something moving inside his skin, from head to toe, without exception, and he could not bear the suffering. He went to see Jiang, but before he arrived, the ghost had already gone ahead. Jiang lived in a house with a tower, and there were dense bamboo groves to the north of the tower. Jiang was sitting on the upper floor when he sensed the presence of the ghost in the bamboo grove. He called out to the ghost and asked questions, and the ghost both informed him of its situation and asked for forgiveness. Jiang said, "I already know." Soon after, the man arrived, and Jiang told him, "You should find an empty house in your village where no one lives, and then come back to tell me." The man went and found such a house. Jiang gave him a piece of paper with his name written on it and instructed him, "When you reach the empty house, discard the paper." Following Jiang's instructions, the man's illness disappeared. There was also a case of someone being troubled by a ghost named Kui. The person placed paintings on the upper floor of their house, but they were all defiled by impurities. They told Jiang about this, and Jiang said, "Just close the upper floor of the house for three days, and the ghost will leave." Following his advice, after three days, the impurities disappeared, and the paintings returned to their original state. I know someone who lives in Shezhou and has personally witnessed these events.

Li Sheng, a scholar, once wandered in the West Mountains of Hongzhou and drank with scholars and companions on a snowy night. One person in the group said, "The snow is so heavy that it is impossible to go out." Li Sheng replied, "Where do you want to go? I can go there." Another person said, "I have books in Xingzi. Can you fetch them for me?" Li Sheng agreed and left to fetch the books. The drinking session had not ended when he returned with the books. Xingzi is about three hundred miles away from the West Mountains. There was a Daoist priest in the temple who once did not show respect to Li Sheng. Li Sheng said, "I cannot kill him, but I will make him fear me." One day, the priest was sleeping in his room, and Li Sheng had a child knock on the door and take out a small dagger. The priest woke up and saw a dagger still moving in front of his pillow. From then on, he changed his attitude and showed respect to Li Sheng.

A Poor Man in Jiankang In the east suburb altar gate outside the city of Jiankang, there once lived a man who did not mention his name. He built a small house facing a wilderness water, just enough to shelter his body. Inside the house, there were only a few household items, and nothing else. Every day, he would enter the city, claiming to be begging, but he did not go through the streets and markets. Instead, he merely entered the temple to wander and observe leisurely. People knew about him, and the patrol official reported this to the superior. The superior ordered to trace his origin and asked what he desired. When questioned, he also had no desire. At the time, it was very cold, and the authorities distributed warm clothes to the poor. Seeing that he was extremely thin, they gave him a warm robe, but he refused to accept it. They forced him to accept it, and he then gave it to someone else, which became even more strange. So they followed him, ordered him to move his residence, and observed where he was going. He destroyed the house and moved to the southwest of Xuanwu Lake, into the imperial garden of Zhang Mou, where there were many overgrown and neglected areas, as well as wild water. Again, he built a house by the water to live in. It snowed heavily for several days, and the gardeners did not see his comings and goings, thinking he had frozen to death. When they inspected the house, they found it had collapsed and said, "He must be dead." They then reported to the officials. Upon opening the house, they saw that he was just sleeping soundly in the snow. He woke up, showing no signs of being cold, and then left, and no one knew where he went.

Chen Yunsheng Chen Yunsheng was from Raozhou, and people called him Chen Bannian. He was quiet and introverted from a young age, fond of the Dao. His family had a tradition of hunting, but Yunsheng alone did not eat their meat and also did not engage in conversation with others. At the age of ten, he went to Longhu Mountain to enter the Dao, dwelling in seclusion in a profound place, few people could see him. Family members, if they saw him, would run away without looking back. During the Tianyou era, he was seen on Magu Mountain in Fuzhou, and it was calculated that he had been away from home for seventy years, yet his appearance remained unchanged. During the Shengyuan era, Governor Wei Quanfeng, when young, knew of his extraordinary qualities and welcomed him to the prefecture. He lived alone in a room, sometimes disappearing. Once, while sitting at night, Wei said to him, "The oranges of Fengcheng are beautiful, I greatly miss them." Yunsheng replied, "There is a boat full of oranges docked at Laocheng Port, I will go and get them for you." The port was fifteen li from the city, and he returned soon, carrying a cloth bag with several hundred oranges, so they ate them together. Wei once needed gold for a wedding, but there was little in the prefecture, not enough to use, and he severely rebuked his subordinates. Yunsheng said, "Do not be angry, I can do it." He took thick paper, coated it with medicine, threw it into the fire, and it all turned to gold, so there was enough to use. Later, Wei fought against the Wu army, and Yunsheng warned him, "Be careful not to put it in your mouth." Quanfeng did not understand the warning, and indeed was defeated at Xiangyata.

Chen Shu, a Daoist from the Shantang View in Qizhou, was said to be over a hundred years old, though his exact age was unknown. He could walk hundreds of miles in a day. Whenever there were banquets in the county, a seat was always left empty for him, and people from far and near would surely attend. Emperor Liezu heard of him and summoned him. When the messenger had not yet arrived, Chen Shu suddenly sighed and said, "I am old now. How can I be of any use to the state, and yet I am being summoned in vain?" A few days later, the messenger arrived. He was summoned again, but he still refused to go. During the reign of Emperor Liezu, known as Baoda, Chen Shu often burned incense in his courtyard at night, looked up to the sky, and offered prayers, then wept bitterly. Soon after, military conflicts broke out in the Huai region, and people believed that Chen Shu had foreseen this. Later, he crossed the river and settled in the abandoned Shengxing View in Yongxing. He built a hut and lived alone, often accompanied by tigers and leopards, which were rarely seen by others. When he died, several days passed before his body was placed in a coffin. At that time, his entire body was covered in sweat.

Zhang Xun's Wife Zhang Xun was a general under Wu Tai Zu, the founder of the Wu state. He had a large mouth, and people called him "Big-Mouth Zhang." During Wu Tai Zu's time in Xuanchou, he regularly provided armor to the generals. Zhang Xun received old and inferior armor, which displeased him, and his dissatisfaction was evident in his words and expressions. His wife told him, "This is not worth worrying about. The Duke doesn't know about it, but if he did, he wouldn't give you such inferior armor." The next day, Wu Tai Zu asked Zhang Xun, "How is the armor you received?" Zhang Xun informed him, and Wu Tai Zu then replaced it. Later, when Wu Tai Zu moved to Guangling, he once gave horses to the generals.
